query:
UPDATE lessons SET content = '## Lesson Overview

The Acts of the Apostles, written by Luke as a sequel to his Gospel, narrates the birth and expansion of the early Christian church from Jerusalem to Rome. The book covers approximately 30 years of church history, documenting the work of the Holy Spirit through the apostles, particularly Peter and Paul. Acts serves as the historical bridge between the Gospels and the Epistles.

## Learning Objectives

Upon completing this lesson, students will be able to:
- Trace the geographical expansion of the early church
- Identify the key figures and events in Acts
- Understand the role of the Holy Spirit in the church''s growth
- Explain the transition from a Jewish to a Gentile-inclusive church

## The Day of Pentecost

Acts begins with Jesus'' ascension and the disciples'' wait for the promised Holy Spirit. On the Day of Pentecost, the Spirit descended upon the believers, empowering them to speak in other languages and boldly proclaim the Gospel. Peter''s sermon resulted in approximately 3,000 conversions, establishing the church in Jerusalem.

## The Expansion of the Church

The church expanded in three phases, following Jesus'' commission in Acts 1:8. First, the Gospel spread throughout Jerusalem despite persecution. Second, Philip and others carried the message to Judea and Samaria. Third, through Paul''s missionary journeys, the Gospel reached the Gentile world, eventually arriving in Rome, the center of the empire.

## Key Figures in Acts

Peter dominates the first half of Acts, preaching at Pentecost, healing the lame man, and opening the door to Gentile conversion through Cornelius. Paul dominates the second half, conducting three missionary journeys, establishing churches throughout Asia Minor and Greece, and ultimately traveling to Rome as a prisoner.

## Key Scripture References

- Acts 1:8 - The commission to be witnesses
- Acts 2:42-47 - The early church''s devotion
- Acts 9:1-19 - Paul''s conversion
- Acts 15:1-29 - The Jerusalem Council

## Summary

Acts demonstrates how the Holy Spirit empowered ordinary believers to accomplish extraordinary things for God''s kingdom. The book records the church''s growth from a small group of Jewish believers in Jerusalem to a multinational movement spanning the Roman Empire. Acts provides the historical context for understanding the New Testament Epistles.' WHERE id = 630004;
